title: Fixing issues - SonarQube for IDE Documentation - VS Code
content:
# Fixing issues

## [Quick fixes](https://docs.sonarsource.com/sonarqube-for-ide/vs-code/using/fixing-issues/#quick-fixes "Quick fixes")

Some issues have Sonar Quick Fixes which means that with a single click, SonarQube for IDE will automatically edit your source code to comply with the rule description and fix the issue. Even when a Sonar Quick Fix is not available, SonarQube for IDE provides options in the tooltip to help you fix your code.

While in the explorer window, select the issue in the PROBLEMS view panel or click the lightbulb in the left margin of the VS Code editor to reveal the tooltip exposing one or more of these options:

- **✧˖° Fix with AI CodeFix**: AI CodeFix suggestions are available when running in connected mode with SonarQube Cloud. See the article about [AI CodeFix in your IDE](https://docs.sonarsource.com/sonarqube-for-ide/vs-code/ai-capabilities/ai-codefix/#ai-codefix "AI CodeFix in your IDE") for details.
- **Open description of rule**: opens a new view panel with the detailed rule description, which usually explains why the issue is raised and explains how to fix it.
- **Show all locations for issue**: opens the **SONARQUBE ISSUE LOCATIONS** view panel and highlights additional locations of this issue.
- **Deactivate rule ‘yyy:XXX’**: This action disables the rule in the user’s VSCode settings and is only available when you are *not using Connected Mode*.

  - To reactivate a rule, go to the **SONARQUBE** > **RULES** view in VS Code and click the 3-dots to select **Find Rule By Key**.

![](https://docs.sonarsource.com/Base64-Image-Removed)

![SonarQube for VS Code uses a lightbulb icon as a tooltip to reveal access to actions you can take on issues in your code.](https://assets-eu-01.kc-usercontent.com:443/b1eeb429-d9e0-0100-be87-468f6802040a/31d4245e-cdf4-44a3-8653-be39a33fec30/sq-vscode-show-fixes.png?w=3456&h=2160&auto=format&fit=crop "SonarQube for VS Code uses a lightbulb icon as a tooltip to reveal access to actions you can take on issues in your code.")

If your code violates more than one rule, a set of options will be presented for each instance. An example is shown in the image below.

- SonarQube for VS Code calls out that your container is missing both CPU *and* memory limits with rules [kubernetes:S6869](https://rules.sonarsource.com/kubernetes/RSPEC-6869/ "kubernetes:S6869") and [kubernetes:S6864](https://rules.sonarsource.com/kubernetes/rspec-6864/ "kubernetes:S6864"); the Maintainability and Security of your code is potentially affected.

![](https://docs.sonarsource.com/Base64-Image-Removed)

![If your code violates multiple rules, multiple quick fixes will be shown.](https://assets-eu-01.kc-usercontent.com:443/b1eeb429-d9e0-0100-be87-468f6802040a/b95e6514-4878-4ff8-8c70-04f4239e250a/sq-vscode-multiple-quick-fixes.png?w=1312&h=634&auto=format&fit=crop "If your code violates multiple rules, multiple quick fixes will be shown.")

## [AI CodeFix in your IDE](https://docs.sonarsource.com/sonarqube-for-ide/vs-code/using/fixing-issues/#ai-codefix "AI CodeFix in your IDE")

If you're running in connected mode with SonarQube Cloud, you might see the  icon which means that there is an AI-generated fix suggestion available. Please check the requirements for using [AI CodeFix in your IDE](https://docs.sonarsource.com/sonarqube-for-ide/vs-code/ai-capabilities/ai-codefix/ "AI CodeFix in your IDE").

## [Fixing injection vulnerabilities](https://docs.sonarsource.com/sonarqube-for-ide/vs-code/using/fixing-issues/#fixing-taint-vulnerabilities "Fixing injection vulnerabilities")

**Injection vulnerabilities**  (also called taint vulnerabilities) are [security-related rule](https://docs.sonarsource.com/sonarqube-server/latest/user-guide/rules/security-related-rules/ "security-related rule") issues that are only raised by SonarQube Server and SonarQube Cloud. Due to technical limitations, SonarQube for VS Code can not raise such issues on local analysis and must be running in [Connected mode](https://docs.sonarsource.com/sonarqube-for-ide/vs-code/team-features/connected-mode/ "Connected mode") to sync injection vulnerabilities from the server.

Injection vulnerabilities are distinguished in the **PROBLEMS** panel with the *Latest SonarQube Server/SonarQube Cloud Analysis* tag as shown in the following image. Learn how to fix your injection vulnerability by using the tooltip options:

1. Select **View as Table** for a more detailed view of your issues.

2. In the **PROBLEMS** view, your taint vulnerabilities are easily identifiable by looking at the analysis **Source**.

3. Select one of your taint vulnerabilities open the **SONARQUBE ISSUE LOCATIONS** view.

4. Click on the lightbulb or right-click on the issue shown to select **Show issue details**; it will open the **SonarQube Rule Description**.

5. Find more information under the **How can I fix it?** tab.

![](https://docs.sonarsource.com/Base64-Image-Removed)

![Injection vulnerabilities (also known as taint vulnerabilities) are also shown in the PROBLEMS view.](https://assets-eu-01.kc-usercontent.com:443/b1eeb429-d9e0-0100-be87-468f6802040a/5d394c74-766b-4fd3-a81c-471af90483f7/sq-vscode-fixing-injection-vulnerabilities.png?w=3456&h=2234&auto=format&fit=crop "Injection vulnerabilities (also known as taint vulnerabilities) are also shown in the PROBLEMS view.")

Please see the documentation about [Injection vulnerabilities](https://docs.sonarsource.com/sonarqube-for-ide/vs-code/using/taint-vulnerabilities/ "Injection vulnerabilities") for more information about working with these particular security issues*.*

## [Marking issues](https://docs.sonarsource.com/sonarqube-for-ide/vs-code/using/fixing-issues/#marking-issues "Marking issues")

When running SonarQube for VS Code in connected mode with SonarQube Server 10.2 and newer, it is possible to mark issues before submitting your code for PR analysis.

To mark an issue, go to your issue in the code editor and select the lightbulb to find the **More Actions…** menu. Then select **SonarQube: Resolve issue violating rule \`**<*your rule>***\`** and choose either **Won’t fix** or **False positive** to resolve the new issue. Note that the **More Actions…** menu is *not available* from the **PROBLEMS** panel.

When running in connected mode with SonarQube Server 10.4 or newer, **Won’t Fix** becomes **Accept**.

Marking an issue can be applied to both *new issues* and *known issues*. Marks made on known issues will be reflected on the SonarQube Server server within a few minutes; marks made on new issues will be reflected on the server when a new analysis is run. The option to mark an issue as resolved will not appear if you are connected to an unsupported version of SonarQube (Server, Cloud).

To unmark *all issues not yet known in SonarQube Server*, open the **VS Code Command Palette** and run the command `SonarQube for IDE: Reopen Local Issues for current file`. This command will only affect new issues that were marked before an analysis was run on the server.

### [Requirements for marking issues](https://docs.sonarsource.com/sonarqube-for-ide/vs-code/using/fixing-issues/#requirements-for-marking-issues "Requirements for marking issues")

- SonarQube for VS Code 3.21 or newer.
- Running in connected mode with SonarQube Server 10.2 or newer (Marking issues while bound to a project in SonarQube Cloud is not supported by SonarQube for VS Code 3.21).
- In SonarQube Server, the **Administer Issues** permission must be granted to the user(s).

This feature is not supported for [security hotspots](https://docs.sonarsource.com/sonarqube-for-ide/vs-code/using/security-hotspots/ "security hotspots") and [injection vulnerabilities](https://docs.sonarsource.com/sonarqube-for-ide/vs-code/using/taint-vulnerabilities/ "injection vulnerabilities"). Please see the dedicated documentation for fixing each of those each issue types.
